clean_text: Running for salah. No noise. Alhamdulillah. This is a huge blessing when we are able to uphold the prophet sallall.aii wasallam sunnah and ourselves and in our peers and our neighbors. Alhamdulillah. The next few verses or actually the remaining verses of are Paul speaking about with generalities but specifically were revealed regarding a man referred to most commonly and most commonly known as Abu Jah. Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala begins the chapter of Alak mentioning the incredible amounts of blessings that Allah has bestowed upon the human being. He has created the human being from drops and clots and small lumps of flesh. He puts a soul into this creature. He takes it through stages, very very precise stages. And then he brings forth a creature that is so intelligent that he is able to understand from the words of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. He is able to recite the literal words of Allah. Yet he is a creation and a creature of Allah. A creature of intellect, a creature of free will. A creature that was meant for more than just the material world. A creature that's not just driven by chemicals or driven by biology. It's a creature that has a will, has an intellect, has a higher purpose, and is able through this higher purpose and this knowledge to transcend the physical world around him. A truly incredible creature. The human being is Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala tells us indeed we have greatly honored the son of Adam the child of Adam. This is one of the most important lessons for us to learn from the book of Allah, especially in our time, in our place amongst popular opinions that would have us believe otherwise. And this is one of the most important lessons that we have to teach our children. What What do we have to teach our children? that you are a human being. That is a huge honor and you are unlike any other creature on this earth. You are meant for a high purpose and your life is inherently valuable. This is something which is often overlooked in our society. Why is suicide so prevalent? Why are people by the hundreds every single year, every year, by the hundreds, people come to the Grand Canyon here in Arizona and fall off the side of the cliff trying to take a selfie? Why does that happen? If a person could really think about this, weighing the risks and the benefits, right? I stand at the edge of this cliff. The greatest benefit I'm looking for is I'm going to get a really nice picture of myself. But what's at risk? What am I risking for this picture? I am risking death or permanent disability. That's what I'm risking. And then go ahead and think that that is and determine that that is worth risking their life. We see addiction, drug use. We see people that do stunts where they jump off of buildings or do all of these crazy things risking their lives. Risking their only chance at paradise. Why? For a laugh. Sacrificing their dignity for a laugh. That's the most they'll get out of it. someone will laugh at them or a person's greatest achievement for risking their life is some money or something of that sort. This is where people are at right now. This is where their minds are at. Why is that? Because human beings in this time and in this place they do not value their lives. They don't see themselves as being valuable. And so we see that all of this is for what reason? What's the reason people are risking their lives and limbs? It's for silly things because they don't value their lives. What is one of the greatest reasons they do not value their lives? It is a disbelief. It is not knowing Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. It is that they have been taught that they're just a creature amongst the creatures of earth. Right? They're just a creature from amongst the creatures of earth. It's very interesting when you look at the concept and the theory, although it's really not a theory. It's more of, you know, , , it's more of a tall tale, but this alleged scientific theory of Darwinian evolution, very interesting because what Darwin thought and what his intellect and he was he was a very intelligent man. His theory is well everything began from a common ancestor. Everything started from a common organism and then life slowly slowly slowly started to randomly mutate and started to grow in different directions because these different organisms are seeking sustenance, right? It's a it's the law of supply and demand. So after being bacteria for a really really really long time, some lightning hit the water or a bunch of different theories that have come up over the years and suddenly we have fish swimming around and then the fish decided, you know what, it's really crowded in here and I think that I could go up on the beach there and probably find some food there that nobody is going to be competing with me for. And then finally they started to grow little legs and they walked up on the beach. And then some of the fish looked up at the trees and said, "You know what? It would be great if I had a tail. summary: This lecture discusses the beginning of Surah Al-Alaq and its broader theological implications regarding human dignity and worth. The speaker emphasizes how Allah honors the children of Adam despite their creation from humble origins, contrasting this Islamic perspective with Darwinian evolution theory. The sermon explores how modern society undervalues human life due to secular ideologies, citing examples like suicide, addiction, reckless behavior, and the commodification of human existence. The speaker critiques evolutionary theory as a framework that denies human special status, arguing it has infiltrated both secular and religious institutions, leading to destructive ideologies and a culture that disrespects human life.
suggested_title: Human Dignity in Islam vs. Darwinian Evolution: Understanding Our True Worth
catchy_title: Why Islam Honors Human Dignity Differently
